topic: My home treadmill app shows three thousand and eleven miles run between two and four in the morning on Wednesdays and the treadmill has been unplugged in my basement for eight months
title: My Treadmill App Logged Three Thousand Miles I Never Ran. The Machine Has Been Unplugged for Eight Months. | True Scary Story
description_intro: My iFit app shows three thousand and eleven lifetime miles on a treadmill I have not used in eight months. Every Wednesday between two ten and three forty four in the morning a new run gets logged. The pace is consistent. The mileage is consistent. The machine is unplugged in my basement under a tarp. The breaker for that outlet has been off since October.
description:
My iFit app shows three thousand and eleven lifetime miles on a treadmill I have not used in eight months. Every Wednesday between two ten and three forty four in the morning a new run gets logged. The pace is consistent. The mileage is consistent. The machine is unplugged in my basement under a tarp. The breaker for that outlet has been off since October.

I did the math on the run log before I went down the stairs to check. Eighty seven Wednesday sessions in the last year. Nine point five miles each. Eight hundred and twenty six miles in the last twelve months alone. The lifetime three thousand and eleven figure works out to roughly six years of weekly sessions. The treadmill is six years old. The console keeps a separate user profile I never created, labeled Owner Number Two, created the same week I unboxed the machine in two thousand nineteen.

There is a detail on the belt and on the console that I have only told my brother about. I dragged the machine to the curb for bulk pickup. The app is still logging sessions. Last week it congratulated me on a new ten kilometer personal record. The machine is gone.

#scarystories #truescarystories #horrornarration #analoghorror #scarystoriestotellinthedark #truehorrorstories #creepypasta #foundtapes
tags: scary stories to tell in the dark

sections.narration: I opened the treadmill app on my phone to delete it. I had not touched the icon in eight months and my phone storage was getting tight. The dashboard loaded for a moment and then settled on a summary screen. Lifetime miles three thousand and eleven. Last run logged that same morning between two ten and three forty four. Distance nine point five miles. Average pace twelve minutes per mile. Calories burned nine hundred and four. There was even a small heart rate graph that climbed and plateaued the way a real run would. I stared at the screen for a long time. The treadmill has been unplugged in my basement for eight months under a green tarp. I had not been near it. I had not been near the basement that night.

close shot of a treadmill console under a translucent tarp, the outline of the screen and handrails visible, low light, horizontal 16:9 framing
close detail of a phone screen showing a recent run summary with a heart rate graph and a pace line, dim afternoon basement light, horizontal 16:9 framing

sections.narration: The basement is half finished. Concrete floor sealed with gray epoxy. Drywall on two of the four walls. A drop ceiling above the laundry sink. A single overhead bulb on a pull chain. The workout corner sits in the back, past the water heater. There is a rubber gym mat I rolled out in two thousand nineteen and a NordicTrack treadmill I bought the same week. The model has a touchscreen console and an iFit subscription I paid for monthly until last fall. There is a small wire shelf beside the machine with a folded towel, a bottle of cleaning spray, and a plastic water bottle in the cup holder. The corner used to have a string of warm white lights tacked along the ceiling joists. I took those down when I gave up.

sections.narration: I quit using the treadmill in October two thousand twenty five. The reasons were the usual ones. A bad knee. A long stretch of work travel. A growing dislike of being alone in a basement at five in the morning. I draped a green painters tarp over the console and the belt to keep dust off. I pulled the power cord out of the wall socket and coiled it on the deck. Two weeks later I went to the breaker panel under the stairs and flipped the switch for the basement utility outlets. That circuit has been off since. The pilot light on the console did not come back on. The folded cord did not move. I had not been down there since the holidays except to swap a furnace filter in February, and the tarp had not been disturbed.

sections.narration: I scrolled the run log on the app. The most recent session was the one from that morning. Above it was the previous Wednesday. Above that the Wednesday before. The pattern held all the way back. Every single entry was on a Wednesday. Every single entry started between two ten and two fourteen in the morning. Every single entry ended between three forty and three forty four. The pace held at twelve minutes per mile in every session, give or take five seconds. The distance held at nine point five miles in every session, give or take a tenth. I scrolled for a long time. The log did not stop at last October when I quit. It did not stop at the holidays. It went all the way back to the week I unboxed the treadmill in two thousand nineteen.

sections.narration: I closed the app. I made coffee. I told myself a list of reasonable things. One. The iFit account had probably been syncing with the gyroscope on my phone and reading my late night bathroom walks as a run. Two. There was an old wearable I had paired to the account three years ago that I might never have unpaired. The wearable could still be streaming step counts somewhere in a drawer. Three. The cloud service might have had a bug that duplicated old sessions and back filled fake new ones to keep streaks alive for advertising purposes. I had read about apps doing things like that. I sat at the kitchen counter and worked through these explanations one at a time. None of them accounted for the exact same pace, the exact same distance, and the exact same Wednesday slot.

sections.narration: I opened a new tab and did the math. Eighty seven Wednesdays in the last year, including the holiday weeks. Nine point five miles per session. Eight hundred and twenty six and a half miles in the last twelve months alone. The app lifetime total of three thousand and eleven miles divided by nine point five works out to three hundred and seventeen sessions. Three hundred and seventeen Wednesdays is six years and one month. The treadmill is six years and one month old. I bought it the second week of April two thousand nineteen. The first run in the app log is dated April seventeen, two thousand nineteen, at two eleven in the morning. I did not run that night. I was on a work trip in Phoenix on April seventeen, two thousand nineteen. I have the boarding pass in my email.

sections.narration: I went to the basement. The stairs felt longer than I remembered. The single overhead bulb buzzed when I pulled the chain. The tarp was still on the treadmill, draped exactly the way I had left it. I lifted the corner with two fingers. The power cord was still coiled on the deck. The plug end was still sitting on the rubber mat, not in the wall socket. I walked to the breaker panel under the stairs. The basement utility circuit was still in the off position. The little orange marker on the switch had not moved. I pulled my phone out and refreshed the run log. A new session had started ten minutes earlier. The current distance read one point two miles. The current pace was twelve minutes per mile. The session was still running.

close shot of a phone screen showing an active run in progress with a rising distance counter, dim basement light, horizontal 16:9 framing

sections.narration: I plugged the cord into the wall socket to test whether the console would even turn on. The breaker was still off so nothing should have happened. The console woke immediately. The screen came up at full brightness without the usual boot animation. The home page loaded a user selection menu. Two profiles were listed. The first one was mine, with my name and the small avatar I had picked from the default set in two thousand nineteen. The second profile was labeled Owner Number Two. The creation date listed under the second profile read October fourteen, two thousand nineteen. The avatar for the second profile was the same default avatar I had picked for my own. I have only ever created one profile on that machine. I have never seen the second profile listed before that moment.

sections.narration: I pulled the tarp the rest of the way off. The belt was worn. There was a thin oval of pale rubber down the center of the belt where feet had repeatedly landed, exactly the wear pattern a regular runner leaves. The belt is rated for ten thousand miles. The wear on it looked closer to three thousand. The motor housing under the console was warm to the touch. The handrails had a thin film on them that felt like dried sweat under my fingers. The cup holder on the console had a half drunk plastic water bottle in it. The label on the bottle was a regional spring water brand I stopped buying in two thousand twenty one when the company switched to a new bottle shape. The bottle in the holder was the old shape. The water inside was still cold.

sections.narration: I unplugged the cord again. I dragged the treadmill up the basement stairs the next morning and out to the curb for bulk pickup. The truck took it before noon. I uninstalled the iFit app from my phone the same afternoon. Three days later I reinstalled it just to confirm the account was closed. The app opened to a familiar dashboard. The lifetime total had updated to three thousand and twenty point five miles. The most recent session had run from two ten to three fifty two the previous Wednesday. The pace was a personal best. Last week the app sent me a push notification congratulating me on a new ten kilometer record. I have not opened the app in eleven days. The machine is gone. The basement corner is empty. The breaker for that outlet is still in the off position.
